one_line_takeaway: A GSK/Harvard proof-of-concept wrapping a fine-tuned Japanese->English ICSR-translation LLM in one hard (drug/AE dictionary-mismatch) and two soft (document- and token-level uncertainty) guardrails, showing a defined drug-name-hallucination 'never event' can be caught 100% of the time on a 20-case sample even though the underlying translations remain error-prone (only 35% clinically accurate).
key_finding: The MISMATCH hard guardrail caught 100% of human-identified spontaneously-hallucinated drug names in the 20-case evaluation sample, supporting the claim that a defined 'never event' can be provably eliminated. mt5-xl was the only viable model, reaching fine-tuned per-token perplexity 1.43 (vs 2.72e3 base) and BLEU 0.39 / SacreBLEU 0.44 / WER 0.73. DL-UQ separated anomalous documents from real ICSRs with AUROC 0.80 (three counterexamples). A counterintuitive result: TL-UQ entropy correlated NEGATIVELY with correctness — higher entropy trended toward MORE clinically accurate, fewer-wrong-drug cases (clinical-accuracy strat adj p=0.0031 and 0.043; wrong-drug strat adj p=0.028), which the authors interpret as possible inappropriate model overconfidence on cases it gets wrong. Underlying LLM quality was mediocre: fine-grained Phase-2 review found high error rates — wrong drug name/info 60%, wrong dates/times 64%, incorrect/missing AE 71%, nonsensical phrases 64%, any 'other' error 75%; only 35% of cases were judged clinically accurate overall.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a proof-of-concept suite of three 'semantic guardrails' bolted onto an LLM that is fine-tuned to translate Japanese Individual Case Safety Reports (ICSRs) into English narrative text for pharmacovigilance (PV) processing at GSK. The three guardrails are: (1) MISMATCH, a HARD guardrail that flags drug/adverse-event (AE) names appearing in the source XOR the target text using regex extraction plus dictionary matching (in-house drug dictionary + MedDRA ~28K preferred terms), enforcing a 'never event' that hallucinated/mistranslated drug names cannot pass silently; (2) DL-UQ (document-level uncertainty quantification), a SOFT guardrail using average-pooled encoder embeddings and a k-NN Euclidean distance to a cache of training ICSR embeddings to flag anomalous/non-ICSR documents at intake; (3) TL-UQ (token-level uncertainty quantification), a SOFT guardrail that scores each generated token by the entropy of its predictive distribution to highlight low-confidence spans for human review. The framing of 'never events' (borrowed from clinical patient-safety literature) as errors that must be provably impossible is a conceptual contribution. NOTE: distinguishing hard/structural guardrails, the guardrails-ai library, and prior LLM-guardrail surveys (Dong et al. 2024, ref [13]) are CITED background, not this paper's work.
threat_model: This is a SAFETY/reliability paper, not an adversarial-security paper. The 'threat' is not a malicious attacker but the LLM's own failure modes in a safety-critical medical pipeline: hallucination (e.g., inventing a drug name or a serious AE like liver failure not present in the source ICSR), omission of key safety information, and miscommunicated uncertainty. The consequence is patient harm or misallocated safety-signal resources (false positives/negatives in pharmacovigilance) and regulatory non-compliance. The guardrails are designed as systemic 'protective barriers' to make defined 'never event' errors (hallucinated drug names) impossible to pass to downstream safety professionals, and to route uncertain/anomalous cases to human-in-the-loop adjudication. There is no attacker model, no data-poisoning or prompt-injection adversary considered.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: hard-guardrail work covered only drug-name hallucination, not other never events (e.g., dechallenge/rechallenge or AE-outcome misinterpretation); drug MISSPELLINGS are explicitly NOT caught by the regex/dictionary matching (and not caught when a drug appears both correctly and incorrectly spelled, 'multiple mentions'); token-level UQ is early-stage. Reviewer-added limits: the headline '100% never-event capture' rests on only 20 cases and one error type, and MISMATCH inherits dictionary coverage gaps (nonzero 'missrates', especially for AEs, which overlap common words). The DL-UQ AUROC 0.80 is modest and evaluated on a tiny injected set (25 extraneous docs). The TL-UQ signal is weak, counterintuitive, and self-described as 'subtle.' Internal inconsistency: the text states LLM correctness was rated 2 for 12.6% while Table 3 lists 15.2% (32/210), and text cites 45% 'perfect' LLM clarity while Table 3 shows only 15.0% at score 5. The whole study is single-site, GSK-funded, on non-public data, primarily one language pair (Japanese->English), with a base LLM whose standalone quality (35% clinically accurate) means the guardrails currently mitigate rather than solve deployment risk.

datasets_used: GSK global safety database: >4 million ICSRs over ~20 years (private, non-public)
Fine-tuning corpus: 131,037 ICSR pretraining examples (Japanese 58,855; German 30,370; French 28,548; Spanish 13,264)
OPUS-100 direct-translation pairs: 10,000 each for Japanese, German, French, Spanish
Model-selection validation subset: 7,820 Japanese ICSRs (~13% of Japanese ICSRs)
Expert human evaluation set: 210 Japanese-origin cases (stratified: vaccines / general medicines / specialty)
MISMATCH evaluation: 20 randomly selected cases from the 210
DL-UQ evaluation: 80 train/val texts + 25 injected extraneous docs (14 Japanese Wikipedia, 7 fake case reports, 2 unrelated Japanese, 2 non-Japanese)
Dictionaries: in-house GSK drug dictionary + MedDRA (~28K preferred terms)
quantitative_results: MISMATCH guardrail caught 100% of human-identified spontaneously-hallucinated drug names (20-case sample)
mt5-xl fine-tuned per-token perplexity 1.43 (base 2.72e3); mpt-7B 113 (base 2.20e7); stablelm-japanese 131 (base 1.09e6)
mt5-xl BLEU 0.39, SacreBLEU 0.44, word error rate 0.73
TL-UQ Mann-Whitney U (Bonferroni n=9): clinical-accuracy strat adj p=0.0031 and 0.043; wrong-drug strat adj p=0.028; entropy correlated negatively with correctness

Phase-2 error rates (Table 4): wrong drug name/info 60% (127), wrong dates/times 64% (135), incorrect/missing AE 71% (149), nonsensical phrases 64% (135), other errors 75% (157), LLM contradictions 41% (86), rechallenge/dechallenge errors 6% (13)
Only 35% (73/210) of LLM-translated cases judged clinically accurate
Table 3 Likert: LLM translation 'correct' scored 2 for 15.2% (32); clarity score-5 15.0% for LLM vs 56.7% for human contractor translation
Inter-rater agreement (source clarity) Cohen's quadratic-weighted kappa 0.542
Selected generation config: contrastive search alpha=0.2, top-k=16
baselines_compared: Three base (non-fine-tuned) LLMs vs their fine-tuned versions, by per-token perplexity: mt5-xl, mpt-7b-instruct, stablelm-japanese
LLM-generated translations vs external human contractor 'ground truth' translations (Phase 1/Phase 2 expert Likert + binary criteria)
Google AutoML Translation BLEU interpretation cited as external reference for BLEU 0.39 being 'high quality'
DL-UQ scores of real ICSRs vs injected extraneous/non-ICSR documents
